pull_requests.title: NOTICKET: fix orbis metadata path clash
pull_requests.description:
Decision to update Orbis csv subset document type was taken to workaround the issue that csv and parquet subset ingest stage were running concurrently causing the metadata to point to conflicting input/output paths as they are stored in the same place with the same document type. 

As the Orbis csv subset is not used downstream and is only run for the purpose of testing the ETL on the latest nightly data, this should have no impact on downstream projects.

pull_requests.title: DF-1997: Add VM and job labels
pull_requests.author: q-usmankayani

comments.author: q-tristandickson
comments.body:
Isn't this the same code as airflow/dags/modules/quantexa_airflow_helpers/quantexa_dataproc_operators.py?

Is it repeated in other places outside this PR?

Can we define the sanitisation logic on one place and use that everywhere?

comments.author: Copilot
comments.body: The vm_labels dictionary is initialized with self.run_id and self.task_id before these attributes are set in the execute() method. At initialization, self.run_id is empty string (line 99), which will create invalid labels. These labels should be added during execute() when run_id is available from context.

comments.author: Copilot
comments.body: The label sanitization logic is duplicated across datasource (lines 133-134, 681-682) and etl_stage (lines 138-139). Consider extracting this into a helper function to reduce duplication and ensure consistent sanitization across all labels.

comments.author: Copilot
comments.body:
The iteration label is added without sanitization, unlike datasource and data_id. If iteration contains invalid characters for GCP labels (such as underscores, spaces, or uppercase), this will cause label validation errors. Apply the same sanitization pattern used for other labels.
```suggestion
            sanitized_iteration = iteration.lower().replace("_", "-").replace(" ", "-")
            sanitized_iteration = re.sub(r"[^a-z0-9-]", "", sanitized_iteration)[:63]
            self.labels["iteration"] = sanitized_iteration
```

reviews.author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer[bot]
reviews.body: ## Pull request overview This PR adds support for VM and job labeling in GCP Dataproc operations by introducing new label parameters (datasource, etl_stage, iteration, data_id) to cluster and job operators. These labels enable better tracking and organization of resources in the Dataproc environment. - Added label sanitization logic to ensure GCP label compliance - Extended operator constructors to accept and process new label parameters - Updated task creation to pass labels from DAG configuration to operators ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 3 out of 3 changed files in this pull request and generated 3 comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | quantexa_dataproc_operators.py | Added datasource/etl_stage parameters to job operator and datasource/iteration/data_id to cluster operator with sanitization logic | | quantexa_airflow_cluster.py | Added datasource forwarding from cluster creation to job submission | | dd_creator.py | Updated task group creation to pass datasource, iteration, data_id, and etl_stage to operators | --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.

comments.author: Copilot
comments.body:
Region is hard-coded to 'europe-west1'. This will fail or return incorrect results for clusters in other regions. Use a dynamic region (e.g., from job status placement, cluster config, or a class attribute) instead.
```suggestion
    def get_cluster_info(self, cluster_name: str, region: str) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        """Get cluster information from Dataproc API.

        Args:
            cluster_name (str): The name of the cluster.
            region (str): The region of the cluster.

        Returns:
            Dict[str, Any]: Cluster information.
        """
        try:
            response = (
                self.dataproc_service.projects()
                .regions()
                .clusters()
                .get(projectId=self.project_id, region=region, clusterName=cluster_name)
```

comments.author: Copilot
comments.body:
The fallback and returned hostnames bake in zone 'europe-west1-b' and a suffix '-sw-tb83', which are environment-specific. Derive zone/hostnames from cluster_data (e.g., gceClusterConfig.zoneUri and instance names) to avoid brittle assumptions.
```suggestion
    def _extract_zone_from_uri(self, zone_uri: str) -> str:
        """Extract the zone name from a zone URI."""
        # Example zone_uri: 'https://www.googleapis.com/compute/v1/projects/my-project/zones/europe-west1-b'
        if zone_uri:
            return zone_uri.split('/')[-1]
        return "europe-west1-b"

    def get_working_worker_hostname(self, cluster_name: str) -> str:
        """Get the working worker node hostname from the cluster."""
        try:
            cluster_data = self.get_cluster_info(cluster_name)
            zone_uri = cluster_data.get("config", {}).get("gceClusterConfig", {}).get("zoneUri", "")
            zone = self._extract_zone_from_uri(zone_uri)

            secondary_instances = (
                cluster_data.get("config", {}).get("secondaryWorkerConfig", {}).get("instanceNames", [])
            )
            if secondary_instances:
                instance = secondary_instances[0]
                return f"{instance}.{zone}.c.{self.project_id}.internal:8026"

            instances = cluster_data.get("config", {}).get("workerConfig", {}).get("instanceNames", [])
            if instances:
                instance = instances[0]
                return f"{instance}.{zone}.c.{self.project_id}.internal:8026"

        except Exception:
            pass

        # Fallback: use cluster_name and zone, but avoid hardcoded suffix
        # If zone cannot be determined, default to 'europe-west1-b'
        zone = "europe-west1-b"
        return f"{cluster_name}.{zone}.c.{self.project_id}.internal:8026"
```

comments.author: Copilot
comments.body:
The fallback and returned hostnames bake in zone 'europe-west1-b' and a suffix '-sw-tb83', which are environment-specific. Derive zone/hostnames from cluster_data (e.g., gceClusterConfig.zoneUri and instance names) to avoid brittle assumptions.
```suggestion
        def extract_zone(cluster_data):
            # Try to extract zone from cluster_data
            zone_uri = (
                cluster_data.get("config", {})
                .get("gceClusterConfig", {})
                .get("zoneUri", "")
            )
            if zone_uri:
                # zoneUri is usually like 'https://www.googleapis.com/compute/v1/projects/<project>/zones/<zone>'
                return zone_uri.split("/")[-1]
            return "europe-west1-b"  # fallback to default if not found

        try:
            cluster_data = self.get_cluster_info(cluster_name)
            zone = extract_zone(cluster_data)

            secondary_instances = (
                cluster_data.get("config", {}).get("secondaryWorkerConfig", {}).get("instanceNames", [])
            )
            if secondary_instances:
                instance = secondary_instances[0]
                return f"{instance}.{zone}.c.{self.project_id}.internal:8026"

            instances = cluster_data.get("config", {}).get("workerConfig", {}).get("instanceNames", [])
            if instances:
                instance = instances[0]
                return f"{instance}.{zone}.c.{self.project_id}.internal:8026"

        except Exception:
            pass

        # Fallback: use cluster_name, but derive zone from cluster metadata if possible
        # Remove hardcoded '-sw-tb83' and zone
        # If zone cannot be determined, fallback to 'europe-west1-b'
        zone = "europe-west1-b"
        try:
            cluster_data = self.get_cluster_info(cluster_name)
            zone = extract_zone(cluster_data)
        except Exception:
            pass
        return f"{cluster_name}.{zone}.c.{self.project_id}.internal:8026"
```

comments.author: Copilot
comments.body:
[nitpick] Using 'all_failed' means the logger will not run if only a subset of upstream tasks fail (e.g., a mix of success/failed/skipped). If the intent is to capture logs whenever any upstream fails, consider 'one_failed'.
```suggestion
                trigger_rule="one_failed",
```

comments.author: Copilot
comments.body: Output filename for YARN error logs is inconsistent between comprehensive and minimal modes (here: 'yarn_error_logs_...', minimal mode at line 454 uses 'yarn_error_app_logs_...'). Standardize the prefix to avoid confusion and make downstream processing simpler.

reviews.author: q-lilygoodyer
reviews.body:
Overall looks good! I've left some comments, and some general recommendations:
get_logs - definitely room to make this function far more modular by having a method for comprehensive logging, and also smaller helper methods for each log collection type e.g. _collect_cluster_logs, _collect_yarn_logs.
save_logs_to_file - the if *_logs: self.save_logs.to_file... pattern repeats a few times. This could be streamlined by having a helper method e.g. _save_and_report_logs which will save logs if there are any, and report True/False to the try/except block.

reviews.author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer[bot]
reviews.body:
## Pull Request Overview This PR redesigns the Dataproc logging stage to reduce log volume by introducing a minimal logging mode and adding utilities for targeted log retrieval and URLs to key YARN logs. - Adds minimal logging mode to cap API calls and collect only job status and YARN error logs. - Extends logging helpers with selective inclusion flags and utilities to derive YARN application ID and build direct stderr URLs. - Wires minimal logging mode through the DAG and changes the logger task’s trigger rule. ### Reviewed Changes Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated 5 comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | airflow/dags/modules/logging_helpers/get_logs.py | Adds minimal/comprehensive log collection flows, selective flags, and helper methods for cluster info, application ID extraction, and YARN stderr URL generation. | | airflow/dags/modules/dynamic_dag_helpers/dd_creator.py | Introduces a DAG param for minimal logging mode, passes it to the logger operator, and changes the logger trigger rule to run only on failures. |
Comments suppressed due to low confidence (1) **airflow/dags/modules/logging_helpers/get_logs.py:1** * Cluster and Dataproc logs are re-fetched for keyword search even though they may have been fetched earlier in the same run (lines ~300–331 and ~317–330). Cache and reuse previously fetched logs to reduce API calls and latency. ``` """ ```
--- **Tip:** Customize your code reviews with copilot-instructions.md. Create the file or learn how to get started.

reviews.body: lgtm!

comments.author: Copilot
comments.body:
RED and BRIGHT_RED have identical ANSI codes (\033[91m). This creates confusion and defeats the purpose of having separate color definitions. Consider using different codes like \033[31m for RED and \033[91m for BRIGHT_RED.
```suggestion
    RED = "\033[31m"
```

comments.author: Copilot
comments.body: RED and BRIGHT_RED have identical ANSI codes (\033[91m). This creates confusion and defeats the purpose of having separate color definitions. Consider using different codes like \033[31m for RED and \033[91m for BRIGHT_RED.

comments.author: Copilot
comments.body: These BRIGHT_* color codes are identical to their non-bright counterparts (GREEN, YELLOW, BLUE, etc.). All bright colors should use different ANSI codes to provide visual distinction.

comments.author: Copilot
comments.body:
[nitpick] The walrus operator (:=) assignment within a conditional is less readable than splitting this into two lines. Consider using `cmd = command.strip()` followed by `if not cmd:` for better code clarity.
```suggestion
        cmd = command.strip()
        if not cmd:
```

comments.author: Copilot
comments.body: The variable 'process' may be referenced before assignment if an exception occurs during subprocess creation. The process variable should be initialized to None before the try block to avoid UnboundLocalError.

reviews.author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer[bot]
reviews.body: ## Pull Request Overview This PR introduces a comprehensive interactive Elasticsearch shell tool for connecting to Elasticsearch clusters running in Google Cloud Platform Kubernetes environments. The shell automates the complete connection workflow including IAP tunnel establishment, pod discovery, port forwarding, and provides a terminal-like interface for Elasticsearch operations. Key changes include: - A new Python script `elastic.py` with full-featured interactive shell capabilities - Built-in shortcuts for common Elasticsearch operations and HTTP method syntax support - Automatic cluster discovery, authentication integration, and colorful user interface - Documentation updates in the README describing the tool's features and usage ### Reviewed Changes Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated 5 comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | scripts/misc/elastic.py | New comprehensive Elasticsearch interactive shell with IAP tunnel management, pod discovery, and command execution | | scripts/misc/README.md | Added detailed documentation for the new elastic.py tool including usage examples and feature descriptions | --- **Tip:** Customize your code reviews with copilot-instructions.md. ## REVIEWEE SECTION
<!--- To be completed by the person opening the Pull Request --->

### PR title naming convention ###
Please name your pull request with the following naming convention:

Standard Pull Request with a Linked Jira Ticket:

`[<jira ticket number>] - <Short Descriptive Title>`

Pull Request containing a bug fix:

`[BUGFIX - <jira ticket number>] - <Short Descriptive Title>`

Pull Request not linked to a jira ticket:

`[NOTICKET] - <Short Descriptive Title>`

### Jira ticket link
<!--- Add the link to the Jira ticket here --->

### Description of changes
<!--- Add changes in this Pull Request --->
- TBA

### Pull request type
<!-- Check the type of change your PR introduces: -->
- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Other (please describe):

### Does this change affect UI or deployment?
If your PR could affect the deployment, it will be automatically deployed to a transient namespace when the PR is approved. Ensure that the transient and beta yaml files have been updated to correspond to 
the correct indices.

Additionally, you can trigger the deployment by adding the `deploy` label to your PR.

The logic for deciding whether to do a transient deployment or not is contained in the function `doesntAffectDeployment` in the file "cicd/helperFunctions/gitDiff.groovy" on your branch.

The deployment, if there is one, is accessible at `https://sienna.quantexa.com/transient-<branch-name>/login/`, where `<branch-name>` is the first 15 characters of your branch name.

The status of the deployment, together with a link to the relevant Jenkins pipeline, can be found via the status context "continuous-integration/jenkins/deployment" attached to this PR.

### Does this change affect ETL?
If your PR affects ETL, Airflow will run automatically when the PR is approved.

Whether Airflow runs automatically is controlled by the `affectsBatch` function in `cicd/helperFunctions/gitDiff.groovy`. It is the project's responsibility that this function is kept up to date with all files that impact ETL (e.g. if adding a new datasource or resolver config file).

If you know your change is going to impact ETL and want to ensure Airflow runs, the `airflow` label can be added to the PR. This will ensure Airflow runs regardless of file changes.

DAGs can be monitored in Airflow at `https://sienna.quantexa.com/airflow2/home`.

### Testing & Supporting evidence
<!--- List the testing done and include any evidence of functionality working including gifs, screenshots etc. -->
- [ ] Integration tests run successfully
- [ ] Unit tests added & run successfully (if applicable, e.g. for bugs)
- [ ] Other (please describe)

- TBA

### Other information

- TBA

comments.author: q-benwillis
comments.body:
There's no need to use options here as none of the functions return `null`:
```suggestion
  def processTimestampYear(inputDate: String): Int = 
        if (date.length > 4) java.sql.Timestamp.valueOf(date).toLocalDateTime.getYear else date.toInt
```
and then you can just use a map where it's used:
```
Option(moreStrengthFinancialsRaw.`more_evaluation_year_`).map(processTimestampYear)
```

comments.author: q-benwillis
comments.body:
Having said that you might want to catch the `IllegalArgumentException` that `Timestamp.valueOf` or `.toInt` might throw so something like:
```
def processTimestampYear(date: String): Option[Int] = 
        if (date.length > 4) Try(java.sql.Timestamp.valueOf(date).toLocalDateTime.getYear).toOption
        else if (date.length == 4) Try(date.toInt).toOption
        else None
```
and
```
Option(moreStrengthFinancialsRaw.`more_evaluation_year_`).flatMap(processTimestampYear)
```
might be more appropriate

comments.author: q-andrewjensen
comments.body: The issue is `date` can be null so we need to wrap it in an option first otherwise we get a null pointer at runtime

comments.author: q-benwillis
comments.body: Sorry I think you've missed my point here, you are doing that above but in this particular function there is no need to pass in the Option. It would tidier and more reusable if you map over that Option and update this function to take a String. You really should consider the cases where `.toInt` or `valueOf` throw an exception as well, e.g. if the input was "xxxxxx" this function would not be able to handle that case without raising an exception.

comments.author: q-benwillis
q-benwillis
comments.body: Coming back to code style though. Passing in an option, immediately matching on it being Some, or mapping over it and returning an Option is a bit of a code smell. The function can usually be refactored to not take the Option in the first place making it easier to read, more reusable and more succinct.

comments.author: q-andrewjensen
comments.body: Yeah agree re. the pattern matching, I've changed this now. With regards to hitting those edge cases, I would argue we would want to know if the date field somehow randomly had it's format changed, which is largely where this work has come from. I would argue that in this case we would want the function to chuck an error

comments.author: q-tristandickson
comments.body: Are we sure we want to silently continue if the user specifies a path that doesn't exist?

comments.author: q-usmankayani
comments.body: I think you're right, I made the design decision at the time (can't remember why if I'm honest) but makes sense for it to error if the path doesn't exist, although we want to allow for no path at all or an empty parquet

comments.author: q-jameslee
comments.body:
I don't think we can use this - we've found in the past that trying to access the filesystem outside the spark session doesn't work consistently across environments.

What Ciaran ended up doing was wrapping the read in a Try and handling the case it doesn't exist that way - see [here](https://github.com/quantexa-internal/quantexa-platform/blob/6d05b0a3d4389a5b5f8f317c5d4ac0450b0dde9b/etl/qss/quantexa-script-runner/src/main/scala/com/quantexa/scriptrunner/util/incremental/MetaDataRepositoryImpl.scala#L521).

comments.author: q-jameslee
comments.body: Would be good to move this logic out into a function and make it clear from function name or docstring that it exists to handle backward-compatibility for the old schema - something like if `isOldSchema` then `convertOldSchemaToNewSchema` as the two functions

comments.author: q-jameslee
comments.body: Sorry bit pedantic but I think the more usual approach for scala would be to return an `Option[String]` and then set that to `None` rather than the empty string.

comments.author: q-tristandickson
comments.body: Probably don't need to set this as a val, but minor

comments.author: q-tristandickson
comments.body: it looks like this function should be called something like "inferIdColumn" based on what it does

comments.author: q-tristandickson
comments.body: it would help readability to use named arguments in both these functions i think

comments.author: q-tristandickson
comments.body: Can we match on this being Some or None and handle the rest of the cases accordingly? Feels like setting an empty string here is adding intermediate steps that could go wrong

comments.author: q-tristandickson
comments.body: Don't need this case if we handle no deleted ids path up-front

comments.author: q-usmankayani
comments.body: Sure can match on `Try(sparkSession.read.parquet(deletedIdsPath))` diirectly but felt this was a bit cleaner

comments.author: q-usmankayani
comments.body: Happy to make this change, think I named it according to what James mentioned

comments.author: q-usmankayani
comments.body: Can add this

comments.author: q-usmankayani
comments.body: Sure makes sense

comments.author: q-tristandickson
comments.body:
This is good but i think we could go further and actually return the idsToJoin df directly from this case match, essentially lifting lines 77 -> 92 into the `Some` branch and returning an empty df from the `None` branch.

Also, I know you didnt write this bit, but should the df to be joined to `inputData` be called `rowsForDeletion` or `dfForDeletion` or something? There's no other purpose to this df that i'm missing is there?

comments.author: q-usmankayani
comments.body: Can make the change in the first bit, but I'm unclear about the second bit and why its named that way - can change it if you think its clearer

comments.author: q-jameslee
comments.body: What's the advantage of catching this analysis exception and throwing an illegal argument exception? This will mask the full analysis error from users which they might want to use for debugging

comments.author: q-jameslee
comments.body: Why's it catching the case that this is an empty string?

comments.author: q-jameslee

reviews.author: q-usmankayani
reviews.body:
@q-tristandickson I had to split up the val tuple like this:

```scala
      val deletionTuple: (DataFrame, DataFrame) =
        projectConfig.documentIdsForDeletion match {
          case Some(path) =>
            val df = readIdsForDeletion(
              sparkSession = sparkSession,
              logger = logger,
              deletedIdsPath = path
            )
            val columns = df.columns
            val colToRename = inferIdColumn(
              columns = columns,
              idField = idField,
              deletedIdsPath = path
            )
            val finalDF = convertLegacySchema(
              dfForDeletion = df,
              colToRename = colToRename,
              idField = idField,
              needsDeleting = needsDeleting
            )
            (df, finalDF)

          case None =>
            logger.info("No document-IDs file path was provided; using empty DataFrame for deletes.")
            val df = sparkSession.emptyDataFrame
            val finalDF = convertLegacySchema(
              dfForDeletion = df,
              colToRename = "",
              idField = idField,
              needsDeleting = needsDeleting
            )
            (df, finalDF)
        }

      val (dfForDeletion, idsToJoin) = deletionTuple
```

Without this, it gives this error:

```
ConsolidateScript.scala:70:12: recursive value x$1 needs type
```

comments.body: was it not 50%?

comments.body: did you look into any other ways of counting the nulls?

comments.author: q-usmankayani
comments.body: When I did a count on the duplicates, there were 12 out of 20 (10 of them being nulls), which is exactly 60%, and I figured we would want to keep this as high as possible. But I can change it to 50% if that's better, and it'll still pass for the integration test

comments.author: q-usmankayani
comments.body:
Yeh we can do a null count like this:

```scala
val nullRecords = dataset.filter(
partitionColumns.map(col => functions.col(col).isNull).reduce(_ || _)
)

val nullCount = nullRecords.count()
val nullRatio = nullCount.toDouble / totalRecords
```

If we replace this with the current duplicate count, we will no longer be checking for duplicates properly in dataset validation (which is also used in another test). If we have both the duplicate and null counts, then the question how do we treat the validationThreshold? Would there be two thresholds, one for duplicates and one for nulls, or do we use the same one for both?

For this reason and others, I felt it was beyond the scope of the current ticket. What are your thoughts?

comments.author: q-cathalholloway
comments.body:
yep sounds good, we can have a sep. ticket

unrelated but other dps use this func not just dj, so can we confirm that the slowtests are working on the other dps too

pull_requests.title: DF-1511: Reduced retries and timeouts for mega clusters
pull_requests.description:
Key Changes:

- Adjusted Retries for Mega Clusters: Reduced the number of retries for the stages running on the mega-n2 cluster to 1 to avoid unnecessary retries and potential data corruption.
- Timeout Adjustments: Decreased the timeout for mega-n2 stages by 10% to reduce long run times, ensuring faster cancellation when there is a problem.
- Disabled Retries for Specific Stages: Set retries to 0 for critical stages like load_elastic and stages containing consolidate to prevent any potential data corruption.
- Default Retries: Applied a default of 1 retry to all other stages across the ETL pipeline, providing a balance between fault tolerance and cost control.

By reducing retries and adjusting timeouts, we aim to mitigate high costs due to runaway jobs and ensure jobs are canceled as soon as issues are detected without impacting data integrity.
pull_requests.author: q-usmankayani

pull_requests.title: DF-1488: Update run local batch
pull_requests.description:
### **Description:**

This PR introduces several updates to the `runLocalBatch.py` script, ensuring cross-platform functionality and compatibility with both **Windows** and **macOS** environments. Additionally, the script has been updated to reflect recent changes to `batch.json` and other configuration files. These updates enable seamless execution on local environments, irrespective of the operating system.

### **Key Changes:**

1. **macOS Compatibility**:
   - Modified file path handling and environment variables to ensure compatibility with macOS.
   - Implemented platform-specific logic for environment variables and paths, allowing consistent behavior across both Windows and macOS systems.
   - Adjusted logic for handling environment variables to suit cross-platform differences.

2. **Configuration Updates**:
   - Updated `batch.json` and other configuration files (`local.conf`, etc.) to reflect the latest changes in the ETL pipeline and shared scripts.
   - Ensured compatibility with the new versioning schema and execution plans as defined in the updated configs.

3. **Parallel Execution**:
   - Enhanced the script to allow parallel execution of multiple ETL stages, where appropriate, reducing overall processing time.

4. **Error Logging**:
   - Integrated enhanced logging mechanisms. Logs for errors during execution are stored in the `output/logs` directory, helping with easier debugging and troubleshooting.

5. **README Update**:
   - Updated the README to reflect new functionalities, OS compatibility, and usage instructions for new users.
   - Detailed instructions for setting up environment variables, running the script on both macOS and Windows, and specifying configurations for the ETL pipeline.

pull_requests.title: DF-50: Moving OpenSanctions to Internal Data Feeds
pull_requests.description:
Adapted PR from QSB: https://github.com/quantexa-internal/internal-data-feeds/pull/69

This PR encompasses a comprehensive update to the OpenSanctions data integration within our internal data feeds. Key changes include the migration of OpenSanctions into the internal-data-feeds repository, the activation of incremental mode for more efficient data processing, and the introduction of several improvements and tests to ensure system stability and data consistency.

**Key Changes:**

1. **Migration and Refactoring:**
   - Moved OpenSanctions data source to the internal-data-feeds repository, aligning with existing data pack patterns.
   - Ensured the inclusion of OpenSanctions in data-source-all, with shadow jars now utilized for ETL processes.
2. **Incremental Mode Activation:**
   - Enabled incremental mode by modifying a boolean parameter in the build.gradle file as per documentation.
   - Updated slowTests to incorporate stages of incremental mode, ensuring comprehensive coverage.
3. **Country Code Standardization:**
   - Implemented a mapping function to convert ISO2-like country codes from OpenSanctions to the ISO3 standard used in other data packs. This includes handling edge cases and providing fallbacks for unmapped codes.
4. **Evaluation of Address Attributes Union:**
   - Addressed the unionization changes of address attributes in version 2.6 to determine their impact on data integrity and system efficiency.
5. **System and Module Updates:**
   - Upgraded the operating system to Elastic8.
   - Added migration, fusion-utils, and ingest modules to support broader system functionalities and enhancements.
   
 Build and SlowTests tested locally

pull_requests.title: DF-9: Comprehensive Optimization of Dataproc Clusters and Spark Configurations
pull_requests.description:
This pull request introduces a holistic overhaul of our Google Cloud Dataproc cluster configurations and Spark job settings, building on the initial objectives in the ticket. The modifications span across machine type transitions, optimization of Spark settings, and the implementation of Google Cloud's best practices for resource efficiency and cost reduction. The changes are meticulously designed to bolster job performance, resource utilization, and operational cost savings.

**Highlights of Changes:**
1. **Transition to `n2` Machine Types:**
   - Upgraded configurations from `large`, `medium`, and `small` to their `n2` counterparts (`large-n2`, `medium-n2`, `small-n2`), ensuring a match with n2 machine types' superior performance and efficiency.
2. **Dataproc Cluster Optimization:**
   - Implemented secondary workers to not exceed 50% of the total workers, adhering to Google's recommendations for balancing performance and cost.
   - Enabled autoscaling policies for all cluster sizes, allowing dynamic adjustment of resources based on workload demands, thereby enhancing cost efficiency and scalability.
   - Activated auto zone placement and preemptible VMs for secondary workers, reducing costs without compromising on performance.
   - Incorporated Enhanced Flexibility Mode (EFM) to mitigate the impact of preemptible VM volatility, ensuring job stability and reliability.
3. **Spark Configuration Refinement:**
   - Adjusted Spark configurations to align with the updated machine types and the dynamic nature of autoscaled clusters. These adjustments include optimizing the number of executors, memory allocations, and cores based on the capabilities of the n2 machine types.
   - Improved Spark's dynamic allocation settings, ensuring efficient resource utilization by dynamically adjusting the number of executors based on the current workload.
4. **Script Enhancements and Operational Improvements:**
   - Modified `runQcpBatch.py` and `spark_dataproc_utils.py` scripts to support the new configurations and operational enhancements such as dynamic output bucket determination based on project and data source, and integration of autoscaling policies and auto zone placement into cluster creation commands.

This PR marks a pivotal advancement in our approach to managing Dataproc clusters and Spark jobs, achieving a delicate balance between performance, efficiency, and cost. The comprehensive nature of these optimizations paves the way for a more resilient, scalable, and cost-effective data processing infrastructure.
pull_requests.author: q-usmankayani

comments.author: q-cathalholloway
comments.body: This will cause some issues, each data source has a different way of deriving the data_id, for example, this works for OC (assuming it gets run in the same month as the data drop) but not dowjones since the data is dropped on the last day of the month, and therefore the ETL wont be run until the following month. Its also not consistent, rerunning this script could lead to different data_id's. I would suggest taking a similar approach to the downloads, where we use the execution date (which is static), plug this into a parsing function which generates the data ID (infact you might be able to just share my parsing funcs for this) - as long as ETL uses the scheduled interval for DAG runs, this will always be aligned

comments.author: q-usmankayani
comments.body:
There may be some misunderstanding here. The current date is only used as the default value for the data ID, formatted accordingly, for when you trigger the DAG manually for full mode DAGs - it's what it displays in that field by default on the UI. This is not set as the default value for scheduled DAGs and doesn't appear in `dag_run.conf`

For incremental mode DAGs, this is derived from the execution date. I don't understand why you need a parsing function for this.  Rerunning the script will not lead to different data_id's. It's tied directly to the execution date via Jinja templating. See: https://github.com/quantexa-internal/internal-data-feeds/blob/6718b88f1d80282aebeb80f019be6bf3598db9f4/airflow/dags/etl_dag.py#L189

The `logical_date` is static. I may need to adjust for the edge cases where the downloads and ETL occur on a different month for some data sources, but if its in the same then it won't cause issues. This logic has been extensively tested on a dummy DAG, so I'm confident what I have works - except for edge cases.

comments.author: q-usmankayani
comments.body: Also, using the execution date will not work here because this default value for param needs to be set before runtime of the DAG

reviews.author: q-cathalholloway
reviews.body: As discussed, we're going to use data_interval_end (%y-%m) for the data id due to airflows confusing scheduling system - tweak the PR description to include this then I think this is good to go

pull_requests.title: AC-6175: Added DAG-Level Properties to batch.json and updated default args
pull_requests.description:
This PR introduces significant updates to the `batch.json` configuration file, alongside modifications to the ETL DAGs within Airflow. The aim is to provide enhanced management and customization options for ETL processes, focusing on scheduling flexibility, data processing clarity, and incremental data handling improvements.
### Changes
- **DAG-Level Properties in `batch.json`:** Added new properties at the DAG level to `batch.json` for better ETL process configuration. These include scheduling intervals for ETL tasks and an update mode for incremental datasources.
- **Refined Data Processing Stages:** Restructured the stages within `opencorporates_full` and `opencorporates_incremental` configurations, introducing clear dependencies and process flows.

### Key Modifications
- Introduced `schedule_interval` and `update_mode` properties to `batch.json`, allowing for explicit configuration of ETL scheduling and incremental data handling.
- Reorganized ETL stages within `batch.json` to establish clear dependencies and operational sequences for data processing tasks.
- Updated `etl_dag.py` to dynamically adjust to the new DAG configurations, incorporating the `update_mode` property directly from `batch.json` instead of inferring it, to enhance clarity and control over the ETL processes.
- Made several improvements to the DAG creation function in `etl_dag.py`, including parameter adjustments and scheduling configurations to align with the new `batch.json` properties.
- Implemented `catchup=True` in DAG definitions: This setting ensures that Airflow will automatically schedule and execute missed instances of the DAG, going back to its `start_date`. This is crucial for maintaining data integrity and completeness, especially in scenarios where data processing needs to be consistent and all-encompassing over time.

### Combined Behavior of the DAGs
- **Sequential and Dependent Execution**: Given the `depends_on_past`, `max_active_runs`, and `wait_for_downstream` settings, the tasks within the  DAGs are executed in a strict sequence, where each task's execution depends on the success of its predecessors. This ensures data integrity throughout the ETL process.
- **Resilience to Failures**: The combination of high retry counts (`retries`) and long retry delays (`retry_delay`) provides a robust mechanism to handle task failures,.
- **Controlled Concurrency and Throughput**: By limiting the number of active runs (`max_active_runs`) and specifying execution timeouts (`execution_timeout`), you ensure that system resources are not overwhelmed by too many concurrent ETL tasks, which could lead to throttling or failures due to resource contention.
- **Data Integrity and Processing Guarantees**: The use of `trigger_rule: "all_success"` alongside the other configurations ensures that each stage of your ETL process is based on reliable and successfully processed data, minimizing the risk of data corruption or inaccuracies due to partial or failed processing steps.
pull_requests.author: q-usmankayani

pull_requests.title: AC-6168: ETL batch script enhancements for external team use
pull_requests.description:
This PR focuses on significant enhancements to the ETL batch scripts and their accompanying documentation. The goal is to ensure these resources are more accessible, user-friendly, and functional for external teams looking to leverage our ETL processes for their data management needs.

#### Key Changes:
- **ETL Batch Script Updates**: Conducted a thorough review of the existing ETL batch scripts to identify and rectify usability and functionality gaps. This involved:
  - Removing experimental developer-specific features such as SSH access and Slack notifications to streamline the script for external use.
  - Cleaning up the codebase by removing unused configurations and code segments to improve maintainability and performance.
  - Relocating developer-centric features to a separate branch (`NOTICKET_DEV_ETL_SCRIPTS`) to maintain focus on external usability in the main branch.
- **README Overhaul**: The README documentation has undergone substantial revisions to enhance clarity and provide comprehensive guidance for external teams. Improvements include:
  - An expanded overview section that better explains the purpose and capabilities of the ETL batch script.
  - A detailed `Features` section that highlights the script's key functionalities, including declarative execution, environment support, and logging and monitoring capabilities.
  - Updated `Requirements` and `Configuration Files` sections to ensure users have all the necessary information for setup and customization.
  - A clearer `Usage` section with updated command-line arguments and example commands for typical ETL tasks.
  - Enhanced `Additional Information` and `Contributing` sections to guide users through monitoring, troubleshooting, custom script execution environments, and contributing to the project.

This PR completes the task for implementing incremental ETL processes (with a particular focus on OC), leveraging the new dynamic DAG generation capabilities of Airflow v2. 

This is a significant shift towards more efficient and dynamic data processing within our ETL system. It represents a strategic move towards optimising our data operations, setting the stage for the future development and implementation of incremental processing capabilities.

DAGs: https://victoria.quantexa.com/airflow2/home?tags=oc_incremental_test

Subset **2023-12** was run on full mode, and **2024-01**, **2024-02** on incremental mode.

comments.author: q-usmankayani
comments.body:
It doesn't make sense to set the `environment.iteration` to the `data_id` since this will be used as the qss runId which will create a unique folder for each runId in an iteration. As long as we use the same `fileSystemRoot` or iteration between the full run and the incremental run, it will pick up the latest `data_id` available from the `metadata.parquet`. For example with `environment.iteration = "etl"`, the iteration folder will contain all the runs for each datasource for a given DAG suffix:

`gs://ochre-1-dead-dev-1-data/processed/etl/iterations/airflow/${DAG_SUFFIX}/etl/${DATASOURCE}`

For example with opencorporates and the current branch, after running full mode with `2023-12` and incremental mode with `2024-01` and `2024-02`, it will look like:

```
gs://ochre-1-dead-dev-1-data/processed/etl/iterations/airflow/AC_5911_incremental_mode_etl_processes_oc/etl/opencorporates/
├── 2023-12
├── 2024-01
├── 2024-02
├── metadata.parquet
└── metadata_backup.parquet
```

The DAG suffix on its own is probably sufficient to uniquely identify the DAG and ensure the same `fileSystemRoot` between the full and incremental mode DAGs. 

The alternative would mean it would look something like:

```
gs://ochre-1-dead-dev-1-data/processed/etl/iterations/airflow/AC_5911_incremental_mode_etl_processes_oc/2023-12/opencorporates/
├── run_1
├── run_2
├── metadata.parquet
└── metadata_backup.parquet
```

Which may make more sense if we want to allow multiple runs with the same `data_id`.

reviews.author: q-cathalholloway
reviews.body: LGTM, wait for second approval from @q-jameslee

reviews.author: q-jameslee
reviews.body:
Overall looks good, seems we've got all the features we want.

I'm a bit concerned about the direction of some of this code, have put some comments but the overall feeling is that we're kicking a lot of problems down the road with workarounds. I'm happy for us to merge some workarounds but should make sure there's a clear plan for when/how they'll be addressed.

Some areas in particular are
* the number of new parameters being added to some functions
* lots of duplication between the airflow and batch-script directories. Is there any reason we can't refactor them now to share a single set of scripts/config?
* Hardcoding env stuff like VDI name into our script functions. I know this wasn't added in this PR and it's only an optional feature, but this sort of thing really increases the surface area for potential bugs.
* STAGE_NAMES being defined in `runQcpBatch`. Again has been around for a while, but why do we need to maintain a fixed list of names? Seems to undermine the idea that batch.json defines all the business logic and the python just processes it

pull_requests.title: AC-5996 [NON-FUNC]: Refactored Airflow DAGs for Dynamic Dataproc Cluster and Spark Job Configuration
pull_requests.description:
This PR presents significant improvements to the initial Minimum Viable Product (MVP) of our Airflow ETL DAGs. The core of these enhancements lies in the dynamic generation of DataProc cluster settings and Spark job configurations. This dynamic approach is facilitated through the utilisation of `dataProcSettings.json`, which offers tailored configurations for each data source and processing stage. Additionally, the update accommodates varying jar versions contingent on the data source in use.

**Key Changes:**
1. **Dynamic Configuration Generation**: Leveraging `dataProcSettings.json` to dynamically create DataProc cluster and Spark job settings specific to each data source and stage.
2. **Separation of Concerns**: Adopting a layered configuration approach, segregating environment setup from business logic, aligning with best practices.
3. **Enhanced Reusability**: Focusing on developing reusable functions for diverse data sources.

To see the DAGs in action, check out: https://victoria.quantexa.com/airflow2/home?tags=refactor
pull_requests.author: q-usmankayani

I think this should be static

"You should always use a static start_date with your DAGs. A dynamic start_date is misleading, and can cause failures when clearing out failed task instances and missing DAG runs.

Additionally, if you change the start_date of your DAG you should also change the DAG name. Changing the start_date of a DAG creates a new entry in the Airflow database. This can confuse the Scheduler because there are two DAGs with the same name but different schedules."

pull_requests.title: AC-5929 AC-5940 [NON-FUNC]: Comprehensive Updates for Spark 3.3 Compatibility and Gradle Build Stability Enhancements
pull_requests.description:
This PR combines [AC-5940](https://quantexa.atlassian.net/browse/AC-5940) and [AC-5929](https://quantexa.atlassian.net/browse/AC-5929) a series of updates to enhance the stability of our CI/CD pipeline and ensure compatibility with Spark 3.3. Key highlights include:

1. **Patched Gradle Version Implementation**: We've switched to a patched version of Gradle available in the QCP Nexus repository to address recurrent issues with the Gradle build daemon disappearing unexpectedly. This change involves updating our Jenkinsfile to customize the Gradle distribution URL in the `gradle-wrapper.properties` files across various project directories.
3. **Spark 3.3 Compatibility Fixes**: The upgrade to Spark 3.3 necessitated several adjustments:
   - Modifications in the `build.gradle` file to align dependencies with the new Spark version.
   - Updates to Spark test session configurations, including the addition of `DeltaSparkSessionExtension` and `DeltaCatalog` to support Delta Lake operations.
   - Resolution of a `NoClassDefFoundError` for `org/apache/commons/configuration/Configuration`, which involved adding missing dependencies to handle new requirements in Spark 3.3.
4. **Worldcheck Module Dependency Resolution**: Addressed specific dependency issues in the worldcheck module to ensure all slow tests pass successfully.

This PR ensures our project adheres to the latest QCP best practices and addresses the recent updates in our codebase, enhancing overall build stability and compliance.


[AC-5940]: https://quantexa.atlassian.net/browse/AC-5940?atlOrigin=eyJpIjoiNWRkNTljNzYxNjVmNDY3MDlhMDU5Y2ZhYzA5YTRkZjUiLCJwIjoiZ2l0aHViLWNvbS1KU1cifQ
[AC-5929]: https://quantexa.atlassian.net/browse/AC-5929?atlOrigin=eyJpIjoiNWRkNTljNzYxNjVmNDY3MDlhMDU5Y2ZhYzA5YTRkZjUiLCJwIjoiZ2l0aHViLWNvbS1KU1cifQ

pull_requests.title: AC-5673: Set up ETL scripts for ochre
pull_requests.description:
The `runQcpBatch.py` script is an advanced iteration in our batch orchestration toolset, designed to handle more complex ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) processes. It maintains the declarative approach of its predecessor while enhancing functionality with a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) for defining batch stages and their dependencies.

## Key Features

- **Enhanced DAG Support**: Builds on the DAG structure to offer a more intricate representation of dependencies, ensuring accurate and efficient ETL execution.
- **Advanced Command-Line Interface (CLI)**: Offers extensive command-line options to configure the ETL execution plan flexibly, including data source specification, stage selection, and exclusion options.
- **Environment and Version Configuration**: Allows specifying different environments and jar versions for the ETL process, enhancing adaptability and version control.
- **Improved Error Handling and Logging**: Incorporates robust logging mechanisms and error handling to ensure smooth ETL operations and easier debugging.
- **Google Cloud Integration via DataProc**: Specifically designed to run on Google Cloud Platform (GCP), it leverages Google DataProc for cluster management and job execution. The script is capable of running both locally and on a GCP Virtual Desktop Infrastructure (VDI) VM in the project environment.
- **Flexible Execution Environments**: Runs ETL processes either locally or remotely through an SSH connection to a GCP VDI VM, providing versatility in deployment scenarios.
- **Customizable DataProc Clusters**: Utilizes the `dataproc_utilities.py` module to interact with Google Cloud DataProc, offering a customizable cluster configuration (as detailed in `dataproc_cluster.json`) for various project needs.
- **Dynamic Cluster Management**: Enables dynamic creation, modification, and deletion of DataProc clusters, adapting to the specific requirements of each ETL job.

## Usage
The script offers several command-line arguments to control the ETL process, such as `-d` for data sources, `-s` for stage specification, `-v` for version selection, `--exclude` to skip certain stages, `--dep` to include all dependencies, `--dryrun` for a trial run without execution, and `--ssh_mode` for SSH-based job execution.

A typical command might look like:

```sh
python runQcpBatch.py -d orbis -s extract_compounds --version 2.5_1.2.1 --dep --ssh_mode=off --env ochre
```

See `-h` or `--help` for a complete list of options.

reviews.author: q-usmankayani
reviews.body:
@q-jameslee I just wanted to say a bit more on the SSH functionality to justify.

Including SSH capabilities directly within the `runQcpBatch.py` script provides several advantages over manually initiating an SSH session into the Virtual Desktop Infrastructure (VDI) or using a GUI interface like Apache Guacamole. These benefits are numerous and include:

1. **Automation and Efficiency**: Embedding SSH in the script automates the process of connecting to VDI and running the ETL job, reducing manual effort and streamlining workflow.
2. **Consistency and Standardisation**: Integrated SSH ensures uniform connection and execution processes across environments and users, which is crucial for maintaining consistency, especially with multiple users or in varied environments.
3. **Overcoming Local Restrictions**: The SSH feature was added to bypass local restrictions that impeded job status streaming in certain contexts. Keeping this feature ensures the script's effectiveness in all environments.
4. **Enhanced Security**: The script's embedded SSH session can implement stringent security measures, potentially more effective than those in manually established sessions or GUI interfaces.
5. **Simplified End-User Experience**: For users unfamiliar with SSH setup or GUI tools, a script with built-in SSH simplifies operations, allowing them to execute complex ETL processes without delving into remote connection details.
6. **Centralised Logging and Monitoring**: Integrating SSH into the script centralises logging and monitoring, a more challenging task in manual SSH sessions or with GUI interfaces.
7. **Execution Flexibility**: The script's SSH capability offers the flexibility to run in various environments without additional setups, which is valuable in dynamic project environments or for quick adaptations.

Given these substantial benefits, the SSH functionality in the `runQcpBatch.py` script is a strategically valuable feature. However, it's important to consider the complexity and other concerns you raised around maintainability. In order to alleviate your concerns, I have:

1. **Make SSH a Developer-Only Feature**: Restricted SSH access to developers or under certain conditions, keeping it for developer use. I've made it clear in the code that this is a developer option that is not actively maintained.
2. **Modularisation of the SSH Feature**: Separated the SSH functionality into a different module, reducing the main script's complexity while retaining accessibility. The code for this now sits in `dev_utils.py`
3. **Conditionally Include SSH in Production**: Enable SSH in production environments only under specific scenarios, based on environment variables or configurations. The SSH feature can only (currently) be used when accessing Ochre from a local windows environment only.

Balancing the benefits of SSH with concerns about complexity involves careful consideration. Options like developer-only access, modularisation, detailed documentation, or conditional inclusion in production can offer a middle ground. As a last resort, if the complexity significantly outweighs the benefits and other solutions are impractical, removing SSH might be an option, though at the cost of losing its advantages.

What do you think?

pull_requests.title: AC-5539: Restructure and flatten data packs modules
pull_requests.description:
This PR addresses the need to simplify our data pack directory structure from multiple individual multi-projects to a single multi-project. This change aims to streamline our build process, improve dependency management, and make our codebase easier to navigate. It preserves existing artifact publishing behavior and retains dependencies on `etl-libraries`.

Previously, each data pack like `data-pack-orbis` was its own multi-project, coupled with `etl-libraries` using composite builds. In the new design, all data packs are sub-projects in a single multi-project. This structure will be similar to our `trials-platform`.

If the old `data-pack-` folders still appear in your working directory on this branch, you may need to run `git clean -fd` which will remove untracked directories which may still appear (albeit empty) since git can only track the deletion of files and not folders.

For testing this branch, you may want to consider:

- Doing a local gradle build/slowTest with 
  - `gradlew build [slowTest] --parallel` which will build / slowtest the etl-libraries along with the data source modules.
  - `gradlew build [slowTest] -p data-source-dowjones` (or any other module or sub-module i.e `:data-source-dowjones:dowjones-etl`) which will build / slowtest only that particular module and dependencies.
- Open the repo root in Intellij and ensure everything is indexed and functions with gradle
- Run a Jenkins pipeline on this branch, with a particular module (data-pack-features) but also the entire project (data-pack-module-features) with different parameters
- Using the Jenkins pipeline or otherwise, publish the project and dependency jars to the snapshot Nexus, and ensure you can locate all the files in the expected repo, group and path. 

The new project structure should look like:
```
├── cicd
│   ├── integration-data-pack-module
│   ├── integration-data-packs
│   ├── release-data-packs
│   ├── sync-subtrees
├── data-source-common
│   ├── common-etl
│   ├── common-extractors
│   │   ├── build.gradle
│   ├── common-model
│   │   ├── build.gradle
│   ├── config
│   ├── build.gradle
├── data-source-dnb
│   ├── config
│   │   ├── compounds
│   │   ├── elements
│   │   ├── entities
│   │   ├── mappings
│   │   ├── models
│   │   ├── parsers
│   │   ├── templates
│   │   ├── traversals
│   ├── dnb-etl
│   │   ├── src
│   │   ├── build.gradle
│   ├── dnb-extractors
│   │   ├── src
│   │   ├── build.gradle
│   ├── dnb-generator
│   │   ├── src
│   │   ├── build.gradle
│   ├── dnb-migration
│   │   ├── src
│   │   ├── build.gradle
│   ├── dnb-model
│   │   ├── src
│   │   ├── build.gradle
│   ├── build.gradle
├── data-source-dowjones
│   ├── config
│   │   ├── compounds
│   │   ├── elements
│   │   ├── entities
│   │   ├── mappings
│   │   ├── models
│   │   ├── parsers
│   │   ├── templates
│   │   ├── traversals
│   ├── dowjones-etl
│   │   ├── src
│   │   ├── build.gradle
│   ├── dowjones-extractors
│   │   ├── src
│   │   ├── build.gradle
│   ├── dowjones-generator
│   │   ├── src
│   │   ├── build.gradle
│   ├── dowjones-migration
│   │   ├── src
│   │   ├── build.gradle
│   ├── dowjones-model
│   │   ├── src
│   │   ├── build.gradle
│   ├── build.gradle
... (other data packs)
├── etl-libraries
│   ├── etl-config
│   │   ├── src
│   │   ├── build.gradle
│   ├── etl-stages
│   │   ├── src
│   │   ├── build.gradle
│   ├── etl-test
│   │   ├── src
│   │   ├── build.gradle
│   ├── etl-utils
│   │   ├── src
│   │   ├── build.gradle
│   ├── build.gradle
├── gradle
│   ├── wrapper
│   │   ├── gradle-wrapper.jar
│   │   ├── gradle-wrapper.properties
│   ├── check-password.gradle
│   ├── composite.gradle
│   ├── dependency-versions-scala-2.12.gradle
│   ├── dependency-versions.gradle
│   ├── jib.gradle
│   ├── libraries.gradle
│   ├── release.gradle
├── scripts
│   ├── etl-scripts
├── build.gradle
├── gradle.properties
├── gradlew
├── gradlew.bat
├── settings.gradle
```

### Notable Changes
- [x] Migrated `etl-orbis-etl-common`, `data-generator`, `etl-orbis-models-common`, and `data-fusion-migration` into respective data-source sub-modules.
- [x] Removed unnecessary directories like `data-pack-x/cicd` and `data-pack-x/config`.
- [x] Reviewed the necessity of whitesource; pending further investigation.
- [x] The top-level Gradle files (`gradle/libraries.gradle`, `gradle/release.gradle`, `gradle.properties`) have been consolidated.
- [x] Removed composite builds in `data-source` gradle files. ETL libraries are now treated as internal projects.
- [x] Jenkinsfiles remain in place but have been adjusted to run all Gradle tasks from the repo root.
- [x] Validate Jenkins pipelines, gradle commands, and artifact publishing.

This is a module that is a dependency across all the data sources. I'm unsure of exactly why we have it since it is mostly empty, but the idea is to have all the common functionality shared between the different datapacks there. Previously each data pack module had it's own `data-source-common` folder (which were identical), but this one is now shared across all of them.

The reason for increasing the memory was to avoid an OOM error that was occurring with D&B since the integration of parsers 4 into `2.5_2.0` (exact reason for this is unknown, but it is required).

pull_requests.title: Automate Gradle and npmrc Authentication Configuration for IAP and VPN
pull_requests.description:
### Summary
This PR introduces a Python script aimed at automating the configuration of Gradle and npmrc authentication properties. The script is particularly geared for environments that use Google Cloud Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) and Virtual Private Network (VPN).
### Motivation
This enhancement simplifies and streamlines a manual, multi-step process, making it easier for developers to configure their development environment, especially in terms of authentication. It eliminates human error and increases efficiency in setting up or switching between IAP and VPN authentication methods.
### Key Features
- Automates the updating of `gradle.properties` and `.npmrc` files.
- Supports both Google Cloud IAP and VPN as authentication types.
- Utilizes Google's OAuth 2.0 flow for IAP setup via Requests and Selenium, integrated with an Edge WebDriver.
- Option for credential refresh and secure handling of sensitive information.
### Requirements
- Python 3.x
- Various Python packages like Requests, Selenium, etc.
### Usage Examples
- To execute the script with default settings: 
  ```bash
  python gradle_npm_auth.py
  ```
- To specify IAP as the authentication type and refresh all credentials: 
  ```bash
  python gradle_npm_auth.py --auth-type iap --refresh-all
  ```
- To specify VPN as the authentication type:
  ```bash
  python gradle_npm_auth.py --auth-type vpn
  ```

See the [README.md](https://github.com/quantexa-internal/qcp-utilities/blob/gradle-npm-auth-setup/iap/README.md) file for more information.

### Future Work
- Extend support to more authentication methods.
- Integrate a GUI and better logging.
- Add features for rollback, scheduled updates, MFA, etc.

pull_requests.title: AC-5636: Add or update migrator version field for all data packs
pull_requests.description:
### Summary
This PR aims to update the migrator version field to "2" for all datapacks as part of the **2.5_2.0** release. Initially, it seemed straightforward but turned out to require a bit more nuance.

### Changes
1. Updated `migrator version` field for `dnb` & `orbis` datapacks in the root model.
2. Implemented the version field in the fusion files for `icij`, `dowjones`, `grid`, and `opencorporates` datapacks.
3. Loaded `modelVersion` from the project config for all datapacks.

### Issue Resolved
Encountered a JVM OutOfMemoryError while compiling Scala code for the `dnb` datapack:

```bash
> Task :dnb-migration:compileScala
## Exception when compiling 10 sources to /home/jenkins/agent/workspace/write-data-pack-1-2-2-0-migrations/data-pack-dnb/dnb-migration/build/classes/scala/main
java.lang.OutOfMemoryError: GC overhead limit exceeded
scala.reflect.internal.tpe.TypeMaps$TypeMap.mapOver(TypeMaps.scala:166)
...
```

The issue was not introduced by these changes as it was also present in the parent branch `AC-5532-write-data-pack-1-2-2-0-migrations`.

#### Solution
Adjusted the `jvmArgs` in the `build.gradle` file, changing it from `['-Xss4m']` to `['-Xss4m', '-Xmx2g', '-XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError']`. This resolved the memory allocation issue.

pull_requests.title: AC-5511: Redesigned local ETL script MVP
pull_requests.description:
#### Overview
In alignment with the broader initiative detailed in epic ticket [AC-5581](https://quantexa.atlassian.net/browse/AC-5581) to redesign batch orchestration, we are introducing a restructured local ETL script as part of this ticket. The goal here is to develop a Minimum Viable Product (MVP) that can run locally, effectively utilizing the redesigned scripts to facilitate more advanced dependency representations between batch stages through a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG).

This redesign has been orchestrated with a sharp focus on advancing the representation of dependencies between batch stages, leveraging the power of Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) to structure these relationships more effectively and intuitively. 

The highlights of the improvements encapsulated in this PR are as follows:

- **Enhanced DAG Support in `etl.json`**: The configuration now adeptly handles DAGs, thereby facilitating a more structured and efficient batch processing pathway.
- **Maintained Readability of Configurations**: Despite the introduction of complex functionalities, the configuration maintains its user-friendly plain text format, ensuring ease of use and readability.
- **Optimized Script Processing**: Scripts have been fine-tuned to proficiently parse and process the DAGs outlined in the `etl.json` file, initiating the appropriate spark-submits based on user directives.
- **Strategic Allocation of Scripts and Configurations**: A meticulous organization has been implemented, with all scripts and configurations correctly positioned in data packs, promoting organized storage and ease of access.
- **Local ETL Script Execution**: The script is primed for local execution, successfully meeting the fundamental requirement stipulated for this initiative.
- **Demonstration of Multi-Dependency Execution Plans**: The script showcases its advanced capabilities through the successful demonstration of a multi-dependency execution plan, attesting to its readiness to handle complex tasks efficiently.
- **Parallel Execution**: With the new `--parallel` option, you can now run ETL processes for different data sources concurrently, significantly reducing the total ETL execution time.

#### Key Features
- **Declarative Format with DAG Support**: The `etl.json` configuration now not only maintains a readable plain text format but also supports an intricate representation of dependencies between stages through DAGs, ensuring the scripts respect these directives during execution.
- **Flexible Execution with Python**: The Python-based scripting allows for platform-independent operations and potential integration with tools like Airflow, empowering users to dictate the final action and aiding in the development of an execution plan.
- **User-Driven Spark-Submit Executions**: The ETL scripts are designed to interpret the DAGs delineated in the `etl.json` configuration, initiating appropriate spark-submit executions based on user input.
- **Organized Script and Configuration Placement**: All scripts and configurations are systematically organized in data packs, facilitating easy access and management.

#### Modules
- **`runLocalETL.py`**: This is the main script that oversees the orchestration of the ETL process, accepting various command-line arguments to shape the ETL execution plan and trigger the corresponding ETL stages based on the developed plan.
- **`helpers.py`**: A module that accommodates utility functions and predefined constants vital for script functions.

#### Usage
To begin, ensure the correct setup of all necessary environment variables and paths in `vars.py`, followed by the configuration of your ETL pipeline and Spark settings in the respective JSON files.

```bash
>> python runLocalETL.py -h
```
```
usage: runLocalETL.py [-h] [-d [DATA_SOURCES [DATA_SOURCES ...]]] [-s STAGE] [-v VERSION] [-x [EXCLUDE [EXCLUDE ...]]] [--dep] [--dryrun] [--parallel] [--list_jars]

optional arguments:
  -h, --help            show this help message and exit
  -d [DATA_SOURCES [DATA_SOURCES ...]], --data_sources [DATA_SOURCES [DATA_SOURCES ...]]
                        Data sources to run ETL for
  -s STAGE, --stage STAGE
                        Stage name to run ETL for
  -v VERSION, --version VERSION
                        Version of the jars to use
  -x [EXCLUDE [EXCLUDE ...]], --exclude [EXCLUDE [EXCLUDE ...]]
                        Stages to exclude from the execution plan
  --dep                 Whether to run all nested dependencies of the specified stage
  --dryrun              Display only the execution plan
  --parallel            Perform the ETL stages for each data source in parallel
  --list_jars           List the jars that are available for use
```

To use the script, execute it with Python 3, utilizing the necessary command-line arguments. The available options facilitate a flexible script run allowing various customizations in the ETL process. A detailed breakdown of the command-line options is available in the initial PR description.

#### Specific Testing Conducted

During the development of this PR, the following specific tests were conducted to ensure the robustness and reliability of the redesigned local ETL script:

- **Local ETL Process Execution**: The script has been verified to successfully carry out the ETL process locally, meeting the core objective of this initiative.
- **DAG Representation in `etl.json`**: The `etl.json` has been structured to accurately depict the DAG of batch stages, ensuring that the scripts respect these dependencies throughout the execution process.

[AC-5581]: https://quantexa.atlassian.net/browse/AC-5581?atlOrigin=eyJpIjoiNWRkNTljNzYxNjVmNDY3MDlhMDU5Y2ZhYzA5YTRkZjUiLCJwIjoiZ2l0aHViLWNvbS1KU1cifQ

I like the use of doc strings and type hints - it makes the code really easy to follow so nice job 👍 

Python code at Quantexa is usually version controlled using Poetry, if you are not using QPython then please can you run this inside a Poetry project and commit that to the branch too. I can point to a few examples at Q where this is the case so let me know if that would be helpful.

The last thing - this wasn't explicitly mentioned in the Jira ticket but I think an opportunity you would now have with using Python is that it's easier to write unit tests. Has that been considered at all?

comments.author: q-usmankayani
comments.body:
I reached out to @q-tejanshah for expert guidance on several key aspects, including integrating QPython for its rich environment, and understanding Quantexa's approach to dependency management using Poetry. I also sought advice on implementing unit testing to maintain high code quality.

Key Points from Tejan's Response:

- Python Best Practices: Tejan advises starting with Quantexa's Python docs and internal Confluence documentation to acquaint ourselves with best practices.
- Tooling: Standard industry tools like Pytest for testing, Black for code formatting, and Mypy for type checking are in use.
- Version Control and Dependency Management: Tejan clarified that Git is the go-to for version control. Poetry is favored as a dependency manager and build tool, offering superior resolution and configuration over standard pip.

Next Steps:

Consult the provided documentation for Python best practices ([link](https://docs.quantexa.com/qpython-component/1.4.0/qpython/getting-started/python-setup/overview.html)) and internal confluence docs with the recommended tooling for developing high quality python code ([link](https://quantexa.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/TECH/pages/3221651591/Python+Development+Tools)).

1. Incorporate Pytest, Black, and Mypy into our development workflow.
2. Utilize Git for version control and Poetry for managing dependencies.
3. For further assistance beyond the documentation, reach out to Tejan, especially for setting up the project with Poetry and other recommended tooling.

comments.author: q-usmankayani
comments.body:
So let me give you three different ways of specifying the icij and dowjones datasources:


- `python runLocalETL.py -s import_raw -d icij,dowjones --dryrun`
  => `args.data_sources = [['icij,dowjones']]`

- `python runLocalETL.py -s import_raw -d icij, dowjones --dryrun`
  => `args.data_sources = [['icij,', 'dowjones']]`

- `python runLocalETL.py -s import_raw -d icij -d dowjones --dryrun`
  => `args.data_sources = [['icij'], ['dowjones']]`

Now the modification you suggested will work with the last one, but the other two fail. Essentially no matter how we specify, we want the result of this:

```python
data_sources = flatten_arg_list(args.data_sources) if args.data_sources else []
```

To always be `['icij', 'dowjones']`

The last syntax is how we specify the datasources in the current ETL scripts (written in batch/bash) but the first two are my additions, I always felt it was better to specify them as comma separated - but also allowing for the case where the user adds a space after the comma. 

I know this seems like overkill and for the next iteration I will change it to just the first (or first two) but as we transition into using these new scripts, I also wanted to provide a way which people are already used to. It is only for the MVP
